item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the image comes from here with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's kind of nonsensical and doesn't explain much but I think that's what I like about it. There's just that vague but damning sense of wrongness and there's no coherent pattern to the "wrongness" or a Big Reveal at the end, it's just there and it's wonderfully unsettling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bit of a necro, but this is my biggest issue with this skip, aside from the bad uses of [REDACTED] and other ambiguity (not that all of them are bad). For one thing, there's no way the Foundation wouldn't be poking and prodding the ever loving hell out of everything they recovered from this anomaly. Looking at the computer hardware in close detail to determine what exactly is wrong with it would be an obvious part of the examination, something that should be mentioned even if we don't see the results in detail.

In general this anomaly seems too sparsely explored and documented for how relatively safe it is, at least compared to other anomalies the Foundation has used as actual permanent facilities. Not to get all "old Foundation", but the loss of 6 agents and a few D-class is nothing compared to the potential benefits of a location like this with a built-in access restriction. They should at least do some more exploring and figure out how truly dangerous the place is before they write it off as something to be contained rather than used. For one thing, that would be the perfect excuse for the author to expand this more, which I think would be welcomed by a lot of people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is like some kid's attempt to run the entire world on the interdimensional equivalent of a 2012 MacBook. There's so much wrong with the world, and that's what makes this skip good.

+1

item.title: Vote - +1
item.content:encoded: I love Places What Are Subtly Creepy and this is one of the best. If I could give you +2, I would, for the Minus World reference. If there were a real-life "Minus World," I'd imagine it'd be something a lot like this.
Yay :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This kind of Reminds me of Contrast where the world is kind of funky.
I would say land a UAV on the tower ^_^ or have a chopper with supplies send a group of people over. Perhaps Establish a base :D Have Guns Loaded with Red Ice and the Contagious Crystal thing on hand.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get it at all, yet finsihing the survey log had me queasy and uncomfortable and I had to upvote it even though the setup and description of the physical aspects around it is a nonsensical jibbering mess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the anomalous space SCPs, especially if they're based on the "corrupted video game map" concept. This one is really neat, but it seems like they don't experiment and explore enough. I'd be sending more drones in to fly around and map out the area in detail. Have one crawl off the end of the world really slowly to see what happens as it goes over, and then pull it back with an attached rope. Analyze the recovered computers further and the Windows already installed on them to see how exactly they differ from others - they don't seem to be just plain defective, if the OS is running.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked and upvoted it, but mayeb it would be more interesting if there were some more pictures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…Voyager 1 is somewhere around 17 light-hours from Earth and we still receive its transmissions.

And that's 40-year-old tech. There's no telling what cutting-edge junk Foundation UAVs are equipped with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoy this entry in large part because it doesn't lay it all out. There is no big, "Ah HA!" moment. It's just a setting. A weird, strange thing that the Foundation can't explain.

That said, Survey 1165-145, has a strange bit in it. Namely, without some interplanetary level comms gear there is no way that drone could still be received. Assuming a nearly human rate of free fall (~120mph), after a year in freefall the drone would be over a million miles away (365 days * 24 hours * 120mph = 1,051,200 miles). It's certainly a creepy tidbit, eternal freefall, but since the Foundation seems to default to normal, modern day equipment, they likely should have lost contact with the drone within a few hours of it going into freefall. Within three months the thing would be farther away than the moon. Years later and it's distance to entrance of 1165 would be measured in light minutes.
Nice work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just realized this reminds me a lot of the Stephen King story "The Langoliers".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And then MTF Tau-1165 was Captain N.

I want to like this - the underlying idea is good and much of the execution is creepy, but it's not working for me. This is probably because I as a read the first parts of it, I expected something like the Rue d'Auseil from The Music of Erich Zann (the Eiffel Tower references helped make this connection). Once I tumbled to the glitched data/video game connection, the whole thing seems… slightly silly.

But, everyone else seems to like it (and as I'm not a frequent contributor), so don't mind me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of the things I like about this are the phrases like "No animals were found, EXCEPT FOR THOSE IN THE LOG .." Way to go. No wonder Smapti's SCPs are my favourite. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yup, I really like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to admit the survey log ends a tad anticlimactically, but what's there is wonderful and I upvoted both the main article and log regardless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one got me. A vast, unpopulated, no man's city.
But why did you need to add the monsters? The almost nostalgic feeling of soaring imagination was immediately spoiled by horror movie scarecrows.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The bit where it consistently disappears all but one of every party that overnights there in unspecified horrific fashion feels gratuitously killy and doesn't seem to fit right. It introduces a near ritualistic killing aspect that feels like it's pointing towards something other than the base concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have the sandbox posted in the forums, it's also on my sandbox page (Boa).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Feel free to use that. :) I can't wait to see it written up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for terrifying me on a gutlevel without me really knowing why. I love the enormous amount of WTF moments in there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, more like some guy's aborted attempt at a level for Duke Nukem 3D. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is horrible and disgusting… you are fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How 'bout a thingy what surgically removes cancerous tumors (with a low rate of failure), then fashions them into decorative knickknacks? Whimsical and horrifying all at once.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a Beta level for the world. Awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How would that even work? a rare form of cancer that forms as a bowl? doesn't seem SCP worthy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So write it up as an SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank god, cancer bowls just send shivers down my spine in the wrong way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Don't worry. While a litte superficially similar to cancer, burls are a completely unrelated phenomenon.

The closest plants get to actual cancer are some bacterial infections that can lead to uncontrolled, undifferentiated cell growth. But the causes aren't related.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Doggerel_bowl.jpg

knowing that this is made out of tree cancer disturbs me a little.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know nothing of botany, do plants get cancer?

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Burl

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: http://www.minecraftwiki.net/wiki/Far_Lands

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Looking back after reading the comments, the junk data concept seems obvious. i mean, go to far in the "level" and you fall through the clipping plane like a 90s FPS, you couldn't have telegraphed it any clearer without an embossed strip-o-gram. But I did not get that at all on first pass. Which is a real pity because I think it could have really turned the creep up to 11.

Maybe you could play up the transcription errors a bit more? A perfect copy of building A, right where it's supposed to be. But 3 blocks over is another building A with the odd and even floors swapped. Or play up that some plants are comletely normal, but some of them are truly crunked-up. I know nothing of botany, do plants get cancer? And does it look as bad as animal tumors can?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would imagine that it's things originating from this place coming out into our world that need to be worried about, but like I said, it's just never gone over in any detail. No doubt anyone who stumbled into it then somehow found their way back out would at least be a little on the crazy side.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This. Very much this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me of a minecraft glitch that happened when infinite worlds were first created: if you got far enough from the center, rounding errors in position tracking would make everything bug out immensely. This feels like that but in real life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Praise from Caesar? :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
creepy in a nice, slow-burn way. This is one that hits not with a hammer, but with a blowdart. You end up thinking about it hours later…what would that place look like? What would it be like to suddenly pop out on some random street like that…

I like it, even if I'm biased to exploration SCP items.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think that's what confused me at first; everyone leaving is hostile/terrified. Not thankful to be back in the real world? Not horrified at what they saw, eager to tell the first person they see not to go in there in a fit of terror?

If there's something terrifying in there, maybe it can go in the exploration log. The weird body thing is clearly not right, but I'm not sure that I would be hostile to normal people upon leaving; I'd be grateful that they had a head and two arms.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I saw the picture and the positive score, and the expectations I got from that were met. The exploration log is great and I think this is sufficiently different from other, similar SCPs that I've read.

One query: containment procedures say to treat anyone exiting the area as hostile, but there's never any real indication of why. I was expecting to see at least an incident log of just such an occurrence, but it never came up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is sufficiently creepy without a big hook, and the work that went into it is impressive too. Still can't work out which SCP it reminded me of at first, but it's definitely standing alone well now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually like this one. It gives me that vaguely unsettling "just not right" feeling one gets in the early part of a (good) Silent Hill game. And I freaking *love* Silent Hill. I don't think this one needs an "Oh!" moment, or some huge reveal; it's just weird, broken chunks of the world, and that's good enough for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved this. It sure as hell grabbed me, but I've been familiar with errors like this since I read glitch lists for N64 games when I was little :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This suggests that there isn't any food to be found in any of the residential buildings.

Or that they ate the food in their building/apartment(s) and decided that going to the convenience store was easier than breaking into others.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Buffer overflow in alleyway() (line 55343). Continue? (y/n) y Floating point error in citygenerator.exe. Continue? (y/n) y

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alright — in this case I would suggest you shrink it down a bit. The wall of text initially made me think that this would be akin to 93, which is what raised my expectations to the point of downvoting when they were not met. I do like the idea of a "junk data" sector, but I think the main article is a bit too bloated right now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm actually hoping this one doesn't reveal everything, it seems like every SCP on the market needs to tell you everything and it ruins the creep for me. I'm much more scared of the things I can imagine more than the things I read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wasn't trying to "connect all the dots together". The similarity to SCP-093 occured to me while I was writing this out and I deliberately decided not to go that route - it would be hard to top and if it didn't work it would just come off as derivative. My objective was simply to establish a setting that's interesting, evocative, and personally terrifying to me, which the Foundation doesn't completely understand.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-093 this aint. I like the idea, but I don't like the fact that ultimately there's nothing there in all of those exploration logs. There's no over-arching theme or storyline to connect all the dots together. The setting is nice, but what's been done with the setting (that is, nothing) is not. Therefore, downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, I think you lose more here in terms of readability than you gain in vagueness. At the least, I'd uncensor the names of SCP operatives.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For the most part, i've only blackboxed the names of people, place names, and dates, because I didn't want to specifically pin down the location of the SCP. I was on the fence about blackboxing the identity of the tall building inside the SCP (it's the Eiffel Tower, for the record), and went with it because I thought explicitly placing it in the story would throw off the tone.

Anything specific that you think shouldn't be censored?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, and you have an upvote. That said, I'd unredact about half of what you have blackboxed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"…was administered a Class-A amnesiac and dumped on the streets"

My I suggest using "relocated" rather than "dumped"? It sounds much better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As has been said before, the junk data idea is cool and should be worked into the article more. At first I thought that it was a kind of Dionaea Street. The article itself was okay, but the log really made it, in my opinion. There wasn't a single "AUGH!" moment, but rather a creeping sense of dread and unease. I liked it a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good points. I made a few tweaks to compensate.

I did specifially intend that radio waves are traveling in and out of the area - the way I see it it sort of "overlaps" the real world in a way. A person who enters the area and walks a kilometer to the west would show up on GPS as being a kilometer to the west of the alley's exit. If you sent someone to that location, though, they wouldn't be there - they'd be at that same geographic position, but inside the SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Any persons entering or exiting SCP-1165 are to be detained, debriefed, and administered a Class-B amnesiac.

This should say "Any unauthorized persons" (or something similar) to make it clear that survey team aren't regularly having their memories wiped upon returning.

Cellular phones and GPS devices continue to function within SCP-1165 and indicate that the device is in a location relative to the distance the bearer has traveled from the western exit of the alley.

So radio waves can travel into and out of the SCP?

Discovering the area to be abandoned, they had taken to squatting in a residential building and scavenging food and alcohol from a building resembling a convenience store.

This suggests that there isn't any food to be found in any of the residential buildings.

Food was found to be fresh and edible,

Hmmm, if I was the Foundation I'd leave behind some surveillance equipment to see if the food was being restocked.

During a survey of the area near the first apparent location of the ██████ █████, a sudden power outage resulted in the loss of cell phone service within the ███████ area.

Why are Foundation exploratory teams using cell phones to remain in contact with mission control? Even if they're cheap, it doesn't seem like a very secure way of communicating.

Also, I found the redactions in the survey log to be frustrating rather than suggestive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) I can't see how that would lead to the "edge of the world" sort of thing as you present it. That is, there being some sort of illusion which covers up the edge until you go past it, being unable to return once past the edge, and there being air and sunlight (but nothing solid) present beyond the edge.

My thinking on that point was sort of related to what happens when you clip past the edge of the world in a video game - you might encounter junk data and nonsense geography for awhile, or you might end up in a bottomless void.

2) Similarly, I can't see how that would lead to copied animal life all becoming nocturnal, aggressive, and impossible to find during the day.

3) Buildings are either copied perfectly or copied such that have noticeably bizarre design and strange looking furniture. If the same happened when copying plant life the results would either have no mutations or be completely nonviable; likewise, copied computers would either be copied perfectly or be completely non-functional. On the other hand, copied plants having lots of mutations suggests that copying causes lots of little mistakes rather than a few large scale mistakes.

I think these go hand-in-hand. The fact that there's so little observable life would probably indicate that most of the "copies" that end up there are nonviable. I didn't go into a lot of description of the plant life, but I think it can be assumed that the plants that are there are stunted and twisted. Since they probably didn't "grow" there, it could be assumed that they came into being in a mutated state that wouldn't have been viable if they'd grown from seed. I didn't want to really go into too many specifics except to say that the objects were "wrong".

I didn't mean to particularly imply that the lifeforms are nocturnal, just that they're rare and hard to find.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Critiques and suggestions regarding the region being made from "junk data":

1) I can't see how that would lead to the "edge of the world" sort of thing as you present it. That is, there being some sort of illusion which covers up the edge until you go past it, being unable to return once past the edge, and there being air and sunlight (but nothing solid) present beyond the edge.

2) Similarly, I can't see how that would lead to copied animal life all becoming nocturnal, aggressive, and impossible to find during the day.

3) Buildings are either copied perfectly or copied such that have noticeably bizarre design and strange looking furniture. If the same happened when copying plant life the results would either have no mutations or be completely nonviable; likewise, copied computers would either be copied perfectly or be completely non-functional. On the other hand, copied plants having lots of mutations suggests that copying causes lots of little mistakes rather than a few large scale mistakes.

4) Presumably the electricity flowing through the power lines in the regions is actively and continuously being copied into the region, rather than being generated within the region. If so, you could the "junk data" think by having the electricity randomly varying from 60 Hz frequency and randomly varying from a perfect sine-wave.
